title: Sperm donors need limits, says a European fertility group
gist: The European fertility body ESHRE is proposing a Europe-wide cap of 50 families per sperm/egg donor (aiming toward 15), arguing that consumer genetic testing has ended donor anonymity and that prolific donors create risks around consanguinity, inherited disease, and psychological harm; enforcement across borders remains the core challenge.

insights.claim: The European Society of Human Reproduction and Embryology (ESHRE) is calling for a Europe-wide limit of 50 families per sperm or egg donor as an initial step, then moving toward 15 families.
insights.speaker: Jessica Hamzelou
insights.so_what: A coordinated regulatory tightening in Europe could constrain sperm-bank supply and volumes; watch commercial banks in exporting countries like Denmark.
insights.quote: ESHRE is calling on sperm and egg banks, as well as fertility clinics, to respect an initial limit of 50 families per donor.

insights.claim: Because donated gametes cross borders, national caps are insufficient and only a transnational limit makes sense.
insights.speaker: Jessica Hamzelou
insights.so_what: Regulatory fragmentation is the crux; effective limits require international coordination that does not yet exist, leaving a persistent enforcement gap.
insights.quote: The only thing that really makes sense is a transnational limit

insights.claim: Experts consider even the proposed 50-family cap very high, and admit no one yet knows the right number, with 15 possibly still too high.
insights.speaker: Jessica Hamzelou
insights.so_what: The proposed limits are a political compromise, not a settled standard; expect continued downward pressure and further regulatory revisions.
insights.quote: We may find that 15 is also too high

insights.claim: Donor anonymity can no longer be guaranteed even where legally permitted, because consumer genetic tests from companies like Ancestry and 23andMe plus genetic registries let donor-conceived people find relatives who share their genes.
insights.speaker: Jessica Hamzelou
insights.so_what: Consumer DNA testing has structurally broken the anonymity model underpinning donation; the industry's legal framework is being outpaced by technology.
insights.quote: Genetic tests offered by companies like Ancestry and 23andMe, along with genetic registries, have made it much easier for donor-conceived people to find parents and siblings who share their genes.

insights.claim: Frozen sperm can be stored for years, so donor-conceived people may discover a genetic parent's identity only after that person's death and find siblings of very different ages across the world.
insights.speaker: Jessica Hamzelou
insights.so_what: Time-shifted usage of frozen gametes compounds tracing and consent problems, a structural feature that limits alone won't fully resolve.
insights.locator: And because sperm can be frozen

insights.claim: Sperm from Dutch donor Jonathan Meijer, who began donating in 2007, was used to conceive between 550 and 600 children before a court ordered him to stop in 2023.
insights.speaker: Jessica Hamzelou
insights.so_what: Illustrates how uncapped donation can produce hundreds of half-siblings, the scale problem driving the regulatory response.
insights.quote: Sperm from Jonathan Meijer, a Dutch man who began donating in 2007, was used to conceive between 550 and 600 children.

insights.claim: A Danish sperm donor carried a genetic mutation significantly raising the risk of multiple cancers, but his sperm had already conceived at least 197 children across Europe, some of whom developed cancer and some of whom died.
insights.speaker: Jessica Hamzelou
insights.so_what: A documented worst-case of inherited-disease propagation at scale; the clearest safety argument for hard per-donor caps despite screening.
insights.quote: But his sperm had already been used to conceive at least 197 children across Europe. Some of those children developed cancer. Some died.

insights.claim: Offspring of a prolific donor risk unknowingly forming romantic or sexual relationships with half-siblings (consanguinity), a key rationale behind population-based donor limits.
insights.speaker: Jessica Hamzelou
insights.so_what: Consanguinity risk scales with offspring count, giving regulators a quantifiable public-health basis for caps.
insights.quote: The offspring of a prolific donor might be at risk of unknowingly forming romantic or sexual relationships, for instance.
insights.locator: Stories like these can be distressing

insights.claim: Existing national limits vary widely, from a single child per donor in Malta and Cyprus, to 10 families in the UK, to 12 families in Denmark.
insights.speaker: Jessica Hamzelou
insights.so_what: The wide dispersion of national rules creates regulatory arbitrage and is precisely why a harmonized European standard is being sought.
insights.locator: Many countries already have legal limits

insights.claim: Denmark is a major sperm exporter: more than half of UK sperm donations in 2020 were imported, most from either Denmark or the US.
insights.speaker: Jessica Hamzelou
insights.so_what: A concentrated cross-border supply chain means Danish and US export policy effectively sets outcomes elsewhere, a chokepoint for both regulation and business.
insights.quote: more than half of sperm donations in 2020 were imported—with most of those coming from either Denmark or the US

insights.claim: Tightening limits could reduce donor-sperm supply and push some prospective parents toward unregulated, unscreened donations that carry health risks and disputes over donors seeking parental rights.
insights.speaker: Jessica Hamzelou
insights.so_what: A supply squeeze creates a second-order risk of a gray market; regulators must weigh caps against driving demand underground.
insights.quote: there's a chance that some people will turn to unregulated sperm donations from people who do not undergo health screening
insights.locator: It will be difficult to enforce

insights.claim: US guidance is far looser: the ASRM cites a suggestion that for a population of 800,000 a single donor be limited to no more than 25 births, though many US banks voluntarily cap at around 25 families per donor.
insights.speaker: Jessica Hamzelou
insights.so_what: The transatlantic gap in standards means international limits face resistance; US banks operate under materially different norms.
insights.locator: And it will be even harder

insights.claim: A donor-conceived advocate argues even five families per donor is too high and international contributions should be capped at two families, while conceding ESHRE's proposal is a positive first step.
insights.speaker: Jessica Hamzelou
insights.so_what: Advocacy groups want far stricter caps than industry, signaling the debate's direction of travel is toward tighter, not looser, limits.
insights.quote: van der Meer thinks that even a limit of five families from a single donor would be high.
